tokens: We show that (4.6 psi) down here.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (reading from the last paragraph on the righthand side of
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: "Verify EVA circuit breaker configuration." On your side (that is, CB panel 11): Suit Fan number 1, Close.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And I'll close the Suit Fan Delta-P (on CB panel 16).
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Turn it off (by opening the circuit breaker).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, and it says "Doff gloves, helmets, visors," and all that; and let's go off relay.
We're going off relay right now, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: How do you want me to go, Al?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Calls at increasing intervals, with no response)
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
Say, are you folks still concerned about the water in the suit loop?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'd like to hear about it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: If you would like to try to get it out, you can blow it out and use the wipe rag that you have over in the aft part of the right-hand equipment stowage bag as a cleanup device.
Or you could probably best put it right over the exit and catch it before you get it into the dust in the cabin.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We're not going to worry about it right now.
We want to get out of all this gear, and we will talk to you later.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: "PLSS Mode (both) zero, connect to LM Comm," (which is a step they did prior to 119:20:50) Okay.
Receive." A is Off; B is Receive.
"Comm VHF, Off, Off, Off, On, Left, High; (Tape) recorder, Off; and Uplink Squelch, Off." All done.
"Verify descent O2 (greater than 35 percent)." Yeah, okay.
"Connect (LM) O2 supply to (PLSS), LMP first." Just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: What are you going to do?
Oh, you don't want a connect yet.
You're going to have to turn around (and) get this side to me.
Yeah, you've got to turn all the way around that way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Wait a minute (while he finds his place in the checklist).
"PLSS fill (valve) Open and then close after 2 minutes." I'll do it on my mark.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Not realizing he is broadcasting) Hey, Al Bean.
That was a hell of a show.
Too bad the TV didn't work.
Would you recycle that stop button?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I can't see it from here, Al.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Although I think you tend to underestimate distances.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You've got to go 2 minutes and you haven't...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You got a false joint in it or something.
It's a good thing the TV wasn't working.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I got a lot of pictures of you around the ALSEP.
I've got good pictures of you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, we're reading you on VOX.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: How far behind time are we?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Aren't we supposed to be doing a '119:45'?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're coming up on a burn with Dick right now, about 4 minutes and 30 seconds away.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Intrepid, plane change burn was good, and we show that you're about 1:15 (one hour and fifteen minutes) behind in the flight plan.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Intrepid, (on) your last step on post-EVA-1 card - where you reverse the O2 hoses - we suggest that's a good time to check for the water.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We are just about at that step right now.
The PLSSs are all recharged (with oxygen) and batteries replaced, LiOH canisters (replaced), and first O2 charge (completed).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause) Pete, our intent on that comment about the PLSS (means 'ECS') O2 hoses is to be assured that those hoses are straight and that you do get all the water running out of them, that there's no low places in the hoses in which the water can lie.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That was a fantastic job you did on picking up the Surveyor and the LM.
Plus the plane change on the burn.
You have been doing a good job.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Houston, we're going to modulate PM.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We just went to modulate PM, per checklist.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, you'll never believe what we've been doing for the last 35 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I am going to take this 35-cent (meaning "cheap") scale that they sent out here to weigh these bags with and break it over somebody's head.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I take it you're having a malfunction with the bag.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Responding to Gibson) No, just the scale.
The nut came off the top of the adjustment; and that's the end of the scale (that is, they can't get it to work).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, we're busy activating the scale experts.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hey, tell me where they stowed the pliers in here.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause) Pete, our first cut (that is, first guess) on the plier location is in one of the PPKs (Personal Preference Kits).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Long Pause while somebody in Houston digs out a stowage list)
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, it's in the lower lunar boot compartment.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Al, how you coming on weighing the water?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Our suggestion is that, if you don't see that you're going to be able to do it in a short period of time, that you move on.
Suggestion is that you look for something where you can accurately measure its volume, and we'll calibrate it when you come back.
And the last alternative is (to) just plain guess at the volume (of water).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, let us think about it a minute.
We're still working on the scale.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: One RCU weighs 3.8 kilograms on this nickel/dime scale.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, if that's what "KG" stands for at the top of the scale.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You had us wondering down here - a few of us.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: This (feedwater collection) bag is really filling up.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Intrepid, for your information, your EVA went 4 hours and 1 minute (from 3.5 psi cabin pressure to 3.5).
And, Al, you were the shortest in terms of quantity remaining.
Your PLSS H2O was down to 47 minutes remaining (an estimate since they are still measuring the residual water); and, Pete, your O2 was most critical on you.
And you had still 2 hours 7 minutes left.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: While we're doing this, what was our BTU output level, do you figure?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I say, what BTU level do you think we were working at?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, you averaged out at 900 BTU; and, Al, you averaged out at 1000.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: What kind of signals are you getting from the ALSEP?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The PSE and LSM are up and working; and they're just going through the activation phases for the remainder.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And, Houston, I'm going to go ahead and top off our PLSSs (with oxygen); and, while we're doing that, I guess we ought to get into our EVA debriefing with you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We also have your lift-off block data for Rev 20 to 24.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Long Pause while Al gets out the Flight Data book so that he can write the times in a pre-printed table) Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Say, Houston, I'm a little bit puzzled about that TV camera.
Do you think it had some sort of mechanical failure or did we point it at the Sun too much?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That's a question right now, and we're trying to figure it out.
We got mechanical and Vidicon-burn people taking sides.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We'll bring it back for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Do you have any questions that you wanted to ask us about the EVA?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'd like to get your comments first if we could, and then we'll take up the questions and recommendations that we can come up with.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: My first comment is that I got water in both my boots, and it's driving me buggy.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: My second comment is that the EVA went pretty well as planned.
I think that most everything - once we got to a task the way we had practiced it back there - we got it done.
It was kind of the unforeseen, as usual, which almost got us behind.
It very definitely took about 10 minutes or so to adapt to what was going on, but as soon as I did, I really got the hot-foot, and I think that Al felt the same way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I think from our end down here, Pete and Al, you did a tremendous job.
You were able to go along, as you said, on the nominal things and take care of the off-nominal also.
There were quite a few points there where we might not have met the objective had you not played "heads up" ball.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Referring to the troubles they'd had getting the fuel element out of the cask) Yeah; that's the handy of having a hammer aboard.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: My heart was in my throat when he couldn't pull that cask out of there.
(Pause) I mean the element out of the cask.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Al, you should have been a surgeon.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: As far as the geology goes...
(Stops to listen to Gibson) That was me that was beating with the hammer, not Al.
(Pause) As far as the geology goes, we really didn't have a chance to look too hard; but I think it's very obvious that there are a variety of different kinds of rocks.
I would also like to say that I think that we're in a most favorable position to get to the Surveyor.
I don't think we want to walk down the crater wall from...
the crater-wall side that the Surveyor is on.
I think what we want to do is walk down in the crater right from the LM across the bottom and walk up to Surveyor.
It looks far too steep to approach from the other side - near the upper part.
Number two, I think that we're pretty well game for any kind of a (geology) traverse that you want us to make.
You know, what we can do here in a few minutes is sit down with our book and put together the best of spot 3 and 4.
And y'all can do the same thing.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're leaning right now towards the
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We show our present thoughts on where you landed are R-2, 15.0.
And, if you like, I'll go ahead and give you the order in which you could hit those points that are spelled out.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'm going to improve your knowledge of where we are.
It just came to me what crater I'm looking into here.
I am sitting approximately 120 feet northeast from the number 3 crater - that's 3 in age - that is on the east side of the Head Crater.
No; as a matter of fact, we're right on about Q-5 and about 14.1.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) The back room will be thinking about that, and we'll get back to you on it.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Now give me some words about the water in my boots.
I've got water in my boots and I want to know what to do about it.
I didn't get any water out of my drain hoses, but I'm just beginning to pick up water in my left boot.
I had it in my right boot for a while.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by, Pete; we will be right with you.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I tell you, the first thing I am going to do is disconnect the suit hoses.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're thinking about that, Pete, and why don't we go on with this debriefing?
And we'll get back to you as soon as we can come up with a good recommendation.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Pete, can we go ahead with the debriefing?
What I'd like to do is give you the recommended order for the points in traverse 4.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: This blue hose is really pumping out pretty moist air.
I'm just going to let it pump it out.
The air is ice-cold air and I think that's part of the problem.
Is there some way we can warm up this air?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Long Pause) Pete, would you give us the position of the suit temperature control valve and also confirm that the LCG pump circuit breaker is pulled?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I guess we'll go to full Hot on that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And the LCG pump breaker is out (meaning that the breaker has been pulled and the pump is off).
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Go ahead; give me your recommended sites now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Number 1 would be F, and that's Head Crater; number 2, B, Bench Crater; number 3, A, Sharp Crater; and we might possibly delete this depending on how you are doing on the timeline at that point.
Number 4 is C, Halo Crater; number 5, D, Surveyor Crater; 6 is E, Block Crater; and we'll omit G.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: (Pause) Oh, it's Sharp Crater, is that right?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we may just cut across that corner depending upon how you are doing with the timeline.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: But don't we also want to get out here on this possible Copernican ray stuff?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We do want to get off after (that is, "go get") that Copernican ray material.

Two points: one is it's further out than you might be able to hack in a normal traverse for the documented samples; and, two, we're not too sure exactly where that line (that is, the edge of the supposed ray) really lies.
If you can, go on over into that area without taking a lot of time away from the other documented sampling; press on.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Now, in looking at the map, we got all the way over to...
If you go to, what is it, the general map, map 5, or whatever you want to call them, we got over in that Shelf Crater (Middle Crescent Crater).
That's where you sent us, and we got to that fellow, so some of that stuff we picked up might be of that Copernican ray material.
We also had photographs down there of that shelf, which everybody thought was interesting.
I took a set of stereos in that thing, all the way around that big crater.
Now, we made it over there with no strain.
Matter of fact, we ran over and ran back in nothing flat.
So, I think it's reasonable to go as you have indicated.

Which would be one, starting at F, which is right in front of the spacecraft, then going to Sharp, then going to Bench, then to Halo, then to the Surveyor crater, then to Block, and back to the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Understand you'd like to go Sharp (Crater) and then Bench (Crater).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, yeah; we can try that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Okay, Pete, if you would, take a look at the information you have there on those sites, and we'll be getting back to you in the pre-EVA briefing and talk a little bit more about the location of the sampling, the core tubes, and the trench site sampling.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: You may have some pretty good ideas on that now, after being able to look at it first hand.
(Pause) And, Pete, we have several questions for you related to the EVA.
We'd like to move through these pretty quickly, as we know we ought to get you off to bed pretty quickly.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: First, a question on the water in the boots.
When was the first time you got water in the boots, Pete; and, Al, do you have any (in yours) at the present time?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I noticed it just starting before...
I just got off the suit loop to Prep for EVA.
It started in my right boot when I came back in.
And these hoses - blue hose, of course - pumped out about three or four three-quarter-inch balls of water when I first disconnected it, just a few seconds ago.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: In the EVA Prep, the PLSS Comm check took longer than nominal.
What corrective action did you take; and do you think we may have a problem the second time around?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: At the front of the RCU, there's a switch, it goes Main, Off, Push-to-Talk or something like that...
And we should have had it at Main and we didn't.
And so we were a little confused there for a while.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: It was entirely an onboard problem.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: How long was our total EVA Prep time?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Pause) Pete, your total EVA Prep time was 2 hours and 8 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I believe we'll do it in about 1 plus 45 tomorrow as planned.
Like Al said: one, we made a couple of mistakes; and the other one, we just had our heads up and locked.
Something we didn't have on the checklist and we should have known better.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: When you put the core tubes in, do you now think it's feasible to join two core tubes together and perhaps get at least one and a half core tube lengths in?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It was getting harder as I drove it in, just like it does back on Earth.
But I think if you wanted to stand there and pound, maybe three times as long as you would have to drive in one, you could do it.
And I don't know if we could do that now, though, with those pins in, but maybe we could take those pins out and put two of them together.
I'd sure be willing to give it a try if you'd want to do it.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We'll be thinking about that one and get back to you with it.
Apparently, the augering is what made the difference there.
If you're looking at your map...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That and you've got to pound the...
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: If you're watching the computer, I'm going to bring it out of Standby and put it back in again to update the clock time.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: There's another one of those downlink too-fast alarms.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Would you give us aft Omni?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: First, would you give us (the) aft Omni (directional antenna)?
And we are ready to pick up with the debriefing.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And, Intrepid, we're ready to pick up with the debriefing.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Two questions related to the mounds which you saw out there.
Is the object at R-5, 13.1 a mound or a rock?
And, secondly, confirm that you did get a sample of the mound material.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We got a sample of the mound material; we got lots of them.
And would you say again the coordinates?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: This mound is too small to show up like that.
I'll look at it a little bit more here for a minute and think about it.
I'll tell you where the mound is.
The mound is not seen on the map.
What you gave me was a crater.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And on that mound sample, you got material from the mound as well as material around the mound itself.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: We can get, tomorrow, a documented sample if you want.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We will talk to you about that in the briefing before the EVA, Pete.
And a question on the number and sizes of rocks.
What was the ratio of fines to rocks that you finally ended up with?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I put two of the large scoops worth of fill (that is, regolith) in one bag that had three rather large rocks in it; I think it's three.
And the other bag of rocks fills half of the rock box, and I guess there were, what would you say, Al, 10 (or) 12 rocks in there?
And the rock box is full to the top.
I couldn't get anything more in there, I'll tell you that, and get the core tube in there.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: One question on the SIDE dust cover.
Was the SIDE dust cover well aligned after reclosing?
And the reason for asking that is in case we suspect a misalignment, we would try to activate that now and if it doesn't work, we'd have a manual backup.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: The SIDE dust cover popped off about three times, Houston.
And the last time it popped off was when we just finished aligning it neatly (and) level and we put out the cold cathode ion gauge.
And the cover popped off again, and we didn't want to disturb the experiment to try and put the cover back on.
We'd spent already overtime on it, so we just left the cover off.
So, as the experiment sets right now, the cover is off.
Now if this is not acceptable, I guess we can take a swing by there tomorrow and try to put the cover back on.
And we can put it on lined up accurately as it was to begin with, if that is what you want.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Stand by on that, and we'll be massaging that one tonight.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: My recommendation is - unless it is going to hurt the SIDE - to leave it just like it is, because it is just a precariously balanced experiment over there and it is going to take time to do it right.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And, Pete, could you give us an estimate of the number of rocks that you have on board?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I really didn't get a count, Houston.
I guess it would be about, (to Al) wouldn't you say, about 15 to 20 rocks is all.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We are looking for really the quantity of rocks, pounds of rocks.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That rock box is heavy, I'll tell you that.
I think it is right up to Max.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: When you took the fuel element out, in the extraction, what was the force profile like?
In other words, did it all of a sudden come free at one point or did it gradually come free as you extracted it?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, Pete started pounding on the side; and, as it came out, an eighth of an inch at a time - make that a sixteenth of an inch at a time - until it was about three-eighths of an inch out.
And once it was three-eighths of an inch out, it slid out rather easily.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: What is the fuel cask made out of?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, I pretty well was beginning to bang it up pretty badly.
As a matter of fact, I think I cracked it.
I had better go look at it tomorrow.
But I was rapping it as hard as I could and was getting about an eighth of an inch at a time until he finally got about, what would you say, Al, an inch and a half out, and then it came all the way.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You don't have to go back to it.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Pete, we have a procedure here in order to get the water out of the suit loop.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, I understand that one, and we'll do it in a little bit.
I got my hoses disconnected anyhow.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we don't see a real easy way of getting the water out of the boots.
If you turn the heat way, way up, we may be able to dry some of it out.
And the other thing is to use gravity in whatever way you could use it.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I don't have that much water in there, and it's drying up.
Or, at least, it's as warm as I am right now, so it's not bothering me and it's no sweat.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I just want to make sure the suit loop's running right, that's all.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Here's a question for you, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We'll be working on that one.
We're going to have a lot of people thinking hard over the sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay, I think we could just call Noun 78 with P20 running.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And the first thing that I'd propose doing, Houston, is we'll get a quick airborne check after we launch and I get a manual lock-on or something, go do a Verb 63, and check the
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We recommend, also, that you shoot for the 3-1/2-hour EVA.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And we'll wake you up at the nominal time in the flight plan (which is 129:55).
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: When do you want us to change the...
Give me a time for the (ECS Primary) LiOH cartridge change, would you, please?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Now we got a couple more questions to get rid of some Irish pennants here; and when you answer these, we can turn you loose to go to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: First off, what are your intentions for your suit-hose configuration for sleep?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Al says he's hot, and he's going to leave his connected to blow air.
I'll probably sleep with mine (his suit hoses) off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Secondly (as per checklist), how about, for our friendly surgeon, a crew status report and a medication and radiation status?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: The crew's in super shape; no medication; and let me look at my little Rad(iation) counter here, just a second.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: (Sounding quite stuffy) LMP had one of those decongestant pills just prior to EVA.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Did you have any problems with the
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We had it in Altitude/Altitude Rate on the descent, naturally; and it looked to me like it agreed pretty closely with the PGNS's numerical readout on the
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I disagree on low transmitter power.
We got surface lockup when the command module went by at a good P22.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And it was not in agreement; it was not erratic in operation in any way, was it?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Nope; it runs just perfect - not erratic.
My impression of these perilune altitude checks, I think, (is that) there may be a bias on it, like 30 or 40 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, the second time you disconnected your suit hoses, did you get any water out of the hoses then?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Well, it was the second time that I got all the water out of them.
I took them off the first time and put them on the floor as advertised and didn't get any water out of them.

I put them back on again; I started getting water in my boots, so I took them off that time, and I never put them on the floor; I just took them off and it blew three three-quarter-inch water balls right out of it, that splattered all over the spacecraft.
And, since then, it's been pretty good.
And we turned the suit-dealie (the suit-circuit temperature control) here up full-hot, and we'll see how that works.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Has Al done the same thing with his hoses, Pete?
And, if he has not, we would like to request that he do so before you turn in.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Yeah, he hasn't gotten any water, but he's going to drain them right now.
Now, look, you got us down here for an hour to eat tomorrow.
We're in one-sixth g and it doesn't take us anywheres near that long to eat; we can whistle through things a lot faster.
I think we can pretty well stick to the nominal timeline and get a good night's rest.
It may turn out, that after 6 or 7 hours worth of sleep, we're going to get stirring because we're both up.
And we're not going to sit here.

So, we'll give you a holler whenever we get up, and we're going to start cooking right then and there and be ready to go over the sill ASAP ("as soon as possible") so we can get as good a EVA out of it as possible and not cut ourselves at the end.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Request that Al go to Suit Flow while he's disconnected so (that), if there is any water in there, it will blow it on out.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: (Expressing his pleasure, with perhaps a small hint of sarcasm) That's a pretty reasonable request.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Pete, can we have the results of Al's suit loop check?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: He got two drops of water.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It's one of those mysteries of life, Paul.
I don't have any water in mine, to speak of, and Pete has his coming out all the time.
We've been shifting over to (Water) Separator B, but I really don't think that's the answer.
Just like on Neil's spacecraft with (garbled) hoses or the way it turns or something else.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And also, on the lithium hydroxide canister change, if it fits in with your activities at that time, we would like to have it changed out at 130 hours on the clock.
And, for your information, you're 12 minutes past the halfway mark in your total mission time.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: And you want the LiOH out at 130:00?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let us know when you're getting ready to turn in, Pete, and we won't bother you any more.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We're still doodling around here a little bit; we're just getting ready to rig the
transcript.speaker: Conrad
